message.content:
Set up S6 (reproducible build) for a Python research pipeline.
1. cd /workspace/POLARIS && git worktree remove /home/polaris/wt/phase-s6 --force 2>/dev/null; git branch -D chore/review-readiness-s6 2>/dev/null; git worktree add /home/polaris/wt/phase-s6 -b chore/review-readiness-s6 chore/review-readiness-phase1.
2. Inventory the current dependency + build state in /home/polaris/wt/phase-s6: requirements.lock / requirements.txt / pyproject.toml / uv.lock / setup.py — which exist, are versions pinned (==), are cryptographic HASHES present (--hash= lines / uv.lock [[package]] hashes)? Count '--hash=' occurrences. Is there an SBOM (any *.cdx.json / *.spdx.json / bom.xml)? Is the build documented (README build section)? Is python version pinned (pyproject requires-python / .python-version)?
3. Check what tooling is available: '/home/polaris/pipeline-env/bin/python -m pip --version', is pip-tools/uv/cyclonedx-py/pip-audit installed ('/home/polaris/pipeline-env/bin/python -m pip list 2>/dev/null | grep -iE "uv|cyclonedx|pip-tools|pip-audit"')?
Return: current state (pinned? hashed? sbom? build-doc? python-pinned?), the count of --hash lines, available tooling, and the gaps to close for S6.

attachment.content:
- deep-research: Deep research harness — fan-out web searches, fetch sources, adversarially verify claims, synthesize a cited report. - When the user wants a deep, multi-source, fact-checked research report on any topic. BEFORE invoking, check if the question is specific enough to research directly — if underspecified (e.g., "what car to buy" without budget/use-case/region), ask 2-3 clarifying questions to narrow scope. Then pass the refined question as args, weaving the answers in.
- artifact-design: Design guidance and fundamentals for Artifacts.
- update-config: Use this skill to configure the Claude Code harness via settings.json. Automated behaviors ("from now on when X", "each time X", "whenever X", "before/after X") require hooks configured in settings.json - the harness executes these, not Claude, so memory/preferences cannot fulfill them. Also use for: permissions ("allow X", "add permission", "move permission to"), env vars ("set X=Y"), hook troubleshooting, or any changes to settings.json/settings.local.json files. Examples: "allow npm commands", "add bq permission to global settings", "move permission to user settings", "set DEBUG=true", "when claude stops show X". For simple settings like theme/model, suggest the /config command.
- keybindings-help: Use when the user wants to customize keyboard shortcuts, rebind keys, add chord bindings, or modify ~/.claude/keybindings.json. Examples: "rebind ctrl+s", "add a chord shortcut", "change the submit key", "customize keybindings".
- verify: Verify that a code change actually does what it's supposed to by running the app and observing behavior. Use when asked to verify a PR, confirm a fix works, test a change manually, check that a feature works, or validate local changes before pushing.
- code-review: Review the current diff for correctness bugs and reuse/simplification/efficiency cleanups at the given effort level (low/medium: fewer, high-confidence findings; high→max: broader coverage, may include uncertain findings; ultra: deep multi-agent review in the cloud). Pass --comment to post findings as inline PR comments, or --fix to apply the findings to the working tree after the review.
- simplify: Review the changed code for reuse, simplification, efficiency, and altitude cleanups, then apply the fixes. Quality only — it does not hunt for bugs; use /code-review for that.
- fewer-permission-prompts: Scan your transcripts for common read-only Bash and MCP tool calls, then add a prioritized allowlist to project .claude/settings.json to reduce permission prompts.
- loop: Run a prompt or slash command on a recurring interval (e.g. /loop 5m /foo). Omit the interval to let the model self-pace. - When the user wants to set up a recurring task, poll for status, or run something repeatedly on an interval (e.g. "check the deploy every 5 minutes", "keep running /babysit-prs"). Do NOT invoke for one-off tasks.
- schedule: Create, update, list, or run scheduled cloud agents (routines) that execute on a cron schedule. - When the user wants to schedule a recurring cloud agent, set up automated tasks, create a cron job for Claude Code, or manage their scheduled agents/routines. Also use when the user wants a one-time scheduled run ("run this once at 3pm", "remind me to check X tomorrow").
- claude-api: Reference for the Claude API / Anthropic SDK — model ids, pricing, params, streaming, tool use, MCP, agents, caching, token counting, model migration.
TRIGGER — read BEFORE opening the target file; don't skip because it "looks like a one-liner" — whenever: the prompt names Claude/Anthropic in any form (Claude, Anthropic, Fable, Opus, Sonnet, Haiku, `anthropic`, `@anthropic-ai`, `claude-*`, `us.anthropic.*`, `[1m]`); the user asks about an LLM (pricing/model choice/limits/caching) — never answer from memory; OR the task is LLM-shaped with provider unstated (agent/MCP/tool-definition/multi-agent/RAG/LLM-judge/computer-use; generate/summarize/extract/classify/rewrite/converse over NL; debugging refusals/cutoffs/streaming/tool-calls/tokens).
SKIP only when another provider is being worked on (overrides all triggers): OpenAI/GPT/Gemini/Llama/Mistral/Cohere/Ollama named in the query; OR `grep -rE 'openai|langchain_openai|google.generativeai|genai|mistralai|cohere|ollama'` over the project hits (run this grep FIRST if no provider named — don't Read the file).
- run: Launch and drive this project's app to see a change working. Use when asked to run, start, or screenshot the app, or to confirm a change works in the real app (not just tests). First looks for a project skill that already covers launching the app; otherwise falls back to built-in patterns per project type (CLI, server, TUI, Electron, browser-driven, library).
- init: Initialize a new CLAUDE.md file with codebase documentation
- review: Review a GitHub pull request; for your working diff use /code-review
- security-review: Complete a security review of the pending changes on the current branch

=== pyproject.toml ===
# Deep Cove Research — tooling configuration.
# Introduced in Phase 0 (code-review readiness). INFORMATIONAL for now: CI runs these
# report-only and does not block. pytest configuration intentionally remains in pytest.ini
# (do NOT duplicate it here — that would change test-collection behavior).

[tool.ruff]
line-length = 100
target-version = "py311"
src = ["src", "scripts"]
extend-exclude = ["third_party", "outputs", "baseline", ".venv"]

[tool.ruff.lint]
# Phase 0 is report-only. We select a broad set but ignore the high-volume "missing docstring"
# codes (tracked separately by interrogate) so the signal is actionable, not a wall of noise.
select = ["E", "F", "I", "B", "UP", "D", "DOC"]
ignore = [
  "E501",  # line length — not enforced during Phase 0
  "D100", "D101", "D102", "D103", "D104", "D105", "D106", "D107",  # missing docstrings: see [tool.interrogate]
  "B008",
]

[tool.ruff.lint.pydocstyle]
convention = "numpy"

[tool.ruff.lint.per-file-ignores]
"tests/**" = ["D", "DOC"]
"scripts/**" = ["D", "DOC"]

[tool.mypy]
python_version = "3.11"
files = ["src/polaris_graph"]
ignore_missing_imports = true
follow_imports = "silent"
# Lenient during Phase 0 — the goal is to get mypy RUNNING in CI, then ratchet later.
check_untyped_defs = false
warn_unused_ignores = false
no_implicit_optional = true

[tool.interrogate]
# Docstring-coverage gate. Floor is intentionally low during Phase 0 (informational);
# ratchet upward toward 85–95 in Phase 3 once the ~530 gaps are backfilled.
fail-under = 50
exclude = ["tests", "scripts", "third_party", "outputs", "baseline"]
ignore-init-method = true
ignore-magic = true
ignore-private = true
ignore-nested-functions = true
verbose = 1

content.text:
I have a complete picture. Let me summarize the S6 state.

**Current state:**
- **Dependency files:** `pyproject.toml` (tooling-only: ruff/mypy/interrogate — no `[project]`, no `[build-system]`), `requirements.txt` (7KB, ~84 `==` pins but with trailing comments and inline notes; hand-maintained, self-documented as non-minimal), `requirements.lock` (uv-compiled, fully pinned: 287 packages, all `==`, zero unpinned), plus several ancillary `requirements-*.txt`. No `setup.py`/`setup.cfg`, no root `uv.lock` (only one under `third_party/DeepResearch-Bench-II/`).
- **Pinned:** YES — `requirements.lock` has all 287 packages pinned with `==` (0 unpinned).
- **Hashes:** NO — 0 `--hash=` lines across every requirements file. The lock was compiled without `--generate-hashes`.
- **SBOM:** NO — no `*.cdx.json`, `*.spdx.json`, `bom.xml`, or `sbom*` anywhere.
- **Build doc:** PARTIAL — README has a "Quick start" section (`python -m venv .venv` + `pip install -r requirements.txt`), but it installs the un-hashed, hand-maintained `requirements.txt`, NOT the pinned `requirements.lock`. No dedicated reproducible-build section.
- **Python pinned:** WEAK — no `.python-version`, no `requires-python` in pyproject (no `[project]` table). Python 3.11 is only pinned implicitly via the uv-compile comment in the lock header (`--python-version 3.11`) and `target-version="py311"`/`python_version="3.11"` in tool configs.

**Tooling available (`/home/polaris/pipeline-env`):** pip 24.2, Python 3.11.10. NOT installed: uv, pip-tools, cyclonedx-py, pip-audit (grep matched only uvicorn/uvloop as false positives).

**Gaps to close for S6:**
1. No cryptographic hashes — regenerate lock with hashes (e.g., `uv pip compile --generate-hashes` or `pip-compile --generate-hashes`).
2. No SBOM — generate a CycloneDX/SPDX SBOM from the pinned lock.
3. No pinned python floor — add `.python-version` and/or `requires-python` to pyproject.
4. Build docs point at the wrong file — README installs un-hashed `requirements.txt`, not `requirements.lock`; needs a reproducible-build section installing the hashed lock with `--require-hashes`.
5. None of the hash/SBOM/audit tooling (uv, pip-tools, cyclonedx-py, pip-audit) is installed in the pipeline env.

input.tooling: pip 24.2 + Python 3.11.10 available at /home/polaris/pipeline-env. NOT installed: uv, pip-tools, cyclonedx-py, pip-audit (grep only matched uvicorn/uvloop as false positives). Note: requirements.lock was originally produced by uv (per header comment) but uv is not present in this env.
gaps: No cryptographic hashes: 0 '--hash=' lines across all requirements files; requirements.lock (287 pkgs, all ==) was compiled without --generate-hashes. Regenerate the lock with hashes and install with --require-hashes.
No SBOM: no *.cdx.json / *.spdx.json / bom.xml anywhere. Generate a CycloneDX or SPDX SBOM from the pinned lock (cyclonedx-py not installed).

Python version not pinned as a floor: no .python-version and no requires-python (pyproject has no [project] table). 3.11 only appears implicitly in the uv-compile header comment and tool.ruff/tool.mypy configs. Add .python-version and/or requires-python.

Build docs point at the wrong file: README Quick start does 'pip install -r requirements.txt' (hand-maintained, self-described as non-minimal, un-hashed) instead of the pinned requirements.lock. Add a reproducible-build section that installs the hashed lock with --require-hashes.
No hash/SBOM/audit tooling installed in pipeline-env (uv, pip-tools, cyclonedx-py, pip-audit all missing) — needed to regenerate the hashed lock and produce the SBOM.
pyproject.toml has no [build-system] and no [project] table — it is tooling-only (ruff/mypy/interrogate); the project is not pip-installable/buildable as a package.
